coredata.dc:title: Ionic liquid-assisted bidirectional regulation strategy for carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/Bi4O5I2 nanomaterials and enhanced photocatalytic properties
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Ji, Mengxia
Xia, Jiexiang
Di, Jun
Wang, Bin
Yin, Sheng
Xu, Li
Zhao, Junze
Li, Huaming
coredata.dc:description: In this study, novel visible-light-driven carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/Bi4O5I2 material has been prepared via a reactable ionic liquid 1-hexyl-3-methylimidazolium iodide ([Hmim]I) assisted bidirectional regulation solvothermal method. This is the first time for the preparation of CQDs/Bi4O5I2 material with halogen and CQDs bidirectional regulation at the same time. With CQDs modified on the surface of Bi4O5I2, fast transfer of photogenerated charges and low recombination of photo-induced electron-hole pairs facilitated the enhancement of photodegradation activity. At the same time, the introduction of CQDs made the electrons occupied in high-energy potential on the conduction band of Bi4O5I2 transfer to the reaction center CQDs and the molecular oxygen can be thus activated. The enhanced mechanisms for the active species (holes, hydroxyl and superoxide radicals) during the photocatalytic reaction under visible irradiation were analyzed using DRS analysis, electron spin resonance (ESR) technique and free radicals trapping experiments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bi4O5I2
Carbon quantum dots
Photocatalytic
Ionic liquid

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21476098 , 21471069 and 21576123 ), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2013M541619 ), Science and Technology support program of Zhenjiang ( SH2014018 , NY2014021 ), and the Science and technology support plan of Dantu ( SH2015002 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of pure Bi4O5I2 and CQDs/Bi4O5I2 samples prepared via a facile solvothermal method.
FT-IR spectra of pure Bi4O5I2 and CQDs/Bi4O5I2 materials.
XPS spectra of Bi4O5I2 and 1wt% CQDs/Bi4O5I2 samples. (a) Survey of the samples; (b) Bi 4f; (c) I 3d; (d) O 1s; (e) C 1s.
TEM images of the as-prepared 3wt% CQDs/Bi4O5I2 materials.
Typical SEM image of 3wt% CQDs/Bi4O5I2 materials and corresponding elemental mapping images of Bi, C, I and O.
The optical absorption spectroscopy of CQDs/Bi4O5I2 and Bi4O5I2 materials.
Nitrogen absorption-desorption isotherm of the as-prepared materials: (a) Bi4O5I2; (b) 0.5wt% CQDs/Bi4O5I2; (c) 1wt% CQDs/Bi4O5I2 and (d) 3wt% CQDs/Bi4O5I2.
Photocatalytic degradation of RhB in the presence of pure Bi4O5I2 and a series of CQDs/Bi4O5I2 materials under visible light irradiation.
Kinetic curves for the degradation of RhB over pure Bi4O5I2 and a series of CQDs/Bi4O5I2 materials under visible light irradiation.
PL spectra for 1wt% CQDs/Bi4O5I2 and Bi4O5I2 samples.
(a) Transient photocurrent responses and (b) electrochemical impedance spectroscopy for Bi4O5I2 and 1wt% CQDs/Bi4O5I2 samples.
ESR spectra of radical adducts trapped by DMPO in CQDs/Bi4O5I2 materials: (a) methanol dispersion and (b) aqueous dispersion under visible light irradiation.
Free radicals trapping experiment of 3wt% CQDs/Bi4O5I2 material under visible light irradiation.
Illustration of the possible reaction mechanism of photocatalytic procedure in the CQDs/Bi4O5I2 materials.

head.ce:title: Ionic liquid-assisted bidirectional regulation strategy for carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/Bi4O5I2 nanomaterials and enhanced photocatalytic properties

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute for Energy Research, Jiangsu University, 301 Xuefu Road, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel CQDs/Bi4O5I2 materials have been prepared via a reactable ionic liquid assisted bidirectional regulation solvothermal method. This is the first time for the preparation of CQDs/Bi4O5I2 material with halogen and CQDs bidirectional regulation at the same time.

In this study, novel visible-light-driven carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/Bi4O5I2 material has been prepared via a reactable ionic liquid 1-hexyl-3-methylimidazolium iodide ([Hmim]I) assisted bidirectional regulation solvothermal method. This is the first time for the preparation of CQDs/Bi4O5I2 material with halogen and CQDs bidirectional regulation at the same time. With CQDs modified on the surface of Bi4O5I2, fast transfer of photogenerated charges and low recombination of photo-induced electron-hole pairs facilitated the enhancement of photodegradation activity. At the same time, the introduction of CQDs made the electrons occupied in high-energy potential on the conduction band of Bi4O5I2 transfer to the reaction center CQDs and the molecular oxygen can be thus activated. The enhanced mechanisms for the active species (holes, hydroxyl and superoxide radicals) during the photocatalytic reaction under visible irradiation were analyzed using DRS analysis, electron spin resonance (ESR) technique and free radicals trapping experiments.

ce:section.ce:para: Solar energy as an essential renewable clean energy has aroused the extensive attention of researchers and how to realize the efficient conversion of solar energy is being explored to achieve a new breakthrough around the world [1–4]. In the last few decades, the semiconductor photocatalysis technology brings us a new discovery of the utilization of sun light, of which anatase phase TiO2 has been regarded as one of the most effective photocatalysts for its predominant photocatalytic activity, high photoelectric conversion efficiency and at a low cost [5–7]. However, the narrow light absorption region of TiO2 greatly limits it to extend the applications to the visible light region [8–10]. Hence, the exploration of visible-light response photocatalysts is indispensable for further practical application.

Bismuth oxyhalides (BiOX, X=Cl, Br, I), as a member of the Sillén family, has drawn extensive interests in photocatalytic researches for its unique layered structure and excellent photocatalytic activities under UV or visible light irradiation [11–19]. The BiOX possessed the structure of [Bi2O2] slabs interleaved with double slabs of halogen atoms could induce the generation of internal static electric fields so as to realize the effective separation of photogenerated carriers and bring about the remarkable photocatalytic activity [20]. Among these, Bismuth oxyiodine (BiOI) has been widely investigated by right of the stronger optical absorptivity in the visible light range. However, the low separation efficiency of photoinduced charge carriers and high recombination rate of photo-generated electrons and holes greatly limited its industrial applications. On account of this, many strategies have been adopted to enhance the photocatalytic activity of BiOI material, such as the control of crystal growth, the regulation of morphology and structure as well as the introduction of defects. At the same time, many of the hybrid materials have also been successfully prepared, for instance, the mesoporous g-C3N4/BiOI [21], AgI/BiOI [22], BiOI/TiO2 [23], BiOCl/BiOI [24], BiOI/n-ZnTiO3 [25] and BiVO4/BiOI [26]. Very recently, a suitable dehalogenation process has been proved to be a useful strategy to further optimize the photocatalytic activities of the BiOX materials. A series of Bi-rich bismuth oxyhalides have been synthesized by different ways, for example, Bi3O4Cl [27], Bi4O5Br2 [28], Bi7O9I3 [29] Bi5O7I [30], and Bi4O5I2 [29,31–33] etc., and the enhanced photocatalytic activity can be observed. Therefore, as if a proper way could be adopted by reasonably regulating and controlling the content of I in BiOI material, the improved photocatalytic activity could be expected.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), with particle sizes below 10nm, have been widely known as a fascinating class of zero-dimensional nanocarbons [34]. The unique properties of stabilities, biocompatibility, nontoxicity and the strong fluorescence promise a potential application for the novel water-soluble carbon nanomaterial. In addition, results of previous researches indicate that CQDs modified on semiconductor photocatalysts can enhance the capacity of light absorption to utilize more of the full spectrum of the sunlight and accelerate the transfer of photo-induced electrons to suppress the rapid recombination of the charge carriers that have been transmitted to the surface of the semiconductors [35]. However, it still needs to explore that how to introduce the CQDs to the semiconductor by evenly disperse it on the surface of the semiconductor.

In the current study, ionic liquid 1-hexyl-3-methylimidazolium iodide ([Hmim]I) was employed to act as reactant source and dispersing agent at the same time to synthesize a novel CQDs/Bi4O5I2 material via a facial solvothermal method. According to our preliminary researches, the introduction of ionic liquid [Hmim]I could promote the CQDs disperse well on the surface of Bi4O5I2 nanosheets so that the fast transfer and efficient separation of the surface charge carriers can be achieved, thus the photocatalytic activity was greatly enhanced [8,36]. In this study, on the one hand, the appropriate removal of halogen atoms in BiOI material could regulate the internal energy band structure of Bi4O5I2 material, so as to the value of downward VB potential could be expected to reach the oxidation reduction potential of oxidizing hydroxyl groups to generate the effective active specie hydroxyl free radical (OH
) in the photocatalytic reaction. On the other hand, the CQDs surface modified Bi4O5I2 material could be expected to rapidly transfer the photo-induced charge carriers, effectively inhibit the recombination of electron-hole pairs and highly efficiently transfer the excited high-energy electrons from conduction band (CB) of Bi4O5I2 material to the surface of CQDs. The bidirectional regulation strategy of energy band modulation and surface modification for the prepared CQDs/Bi4O5I2 material was proposed for the first time. Rhodamine B (RhB) was degraded under visible light irradiation to evaluate the photocatalytic activity of CQDs/Bi4O5I2 materials. And a possible mechanism for the photodegradation process was proposed eventually.

Materials used in experiments were analytical purity and used as yet untreated. The ionic liquid1-hexyl-3-methylimidazolium iodide ([Hmim]I) (99%) was purchased from Shanghai Chengjie Chemical Co. Ltd.

CQDs powder was prepared according to the literature with freeze-drying [34]. 1mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and 1mmol [Hmim]I were dissolved in 10mL mannitol under magnetic stirring to obtain suspension A and B, respectively. Then solution B was added into the A suspension by drops with steadily stirring. After that, different amount of as-prepared CQDs powder was added into the suspension and the pH of the suspension was adjusted to 10 by 1mol/L NaOH solution with continuous stirring for 30min at room temperature. The mixture was poured into a 25mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 140°C for 24h in a drying oven. Subsequently the precipitate was collected from refrigerant autoclave and washed several times with aquadistillate and anhydrous ethanol by centrifugation. The obtained precipitate then was dried at 60°C for hours. The content of CQDs added in CQDs/Bi4O5I2 materials was 0.5wt%, 1wt%, 3wt%, respectively.

Power X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra for the as-prepared photocatalysts were determined using a Shimadzu XRD-6000 X-ray diffractometer with CuKα radiation (λ=1.54Å). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) were measured on a Nicolet Model Nexus 470 FT-IR equipment using KBr pellet technic. UV–vis diffuse refection spectra (DRS) were recorded on an UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2450 Shimadzu Corporation, Japan) in the range of 200–800nm (BaSO4 powder used as the substrate). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was taken on an ESCALab MKII X-ray photo-electron spectrometer using the MgKα radiation. The electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were examined on a Bruker model ESR JES-FA200 spectrometer with the spin-trap reagent DMPO (Sigma Chemical Co.) in methanol and water. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs were observed on a JEOL-JEM-2010 (JEOL, Japan) operated at 200kV. The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms at 77K were researched using a TriStar II 3020 surface area and porosity analyzer (Micromeritics Instrument Corporation, USA).

The photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared CQDs/Bi4O5I2 materials was evaluated by the degradation of rhodamine B (RhB) in solution under visible light irradiation. When photocatalytic reaction was performed, the pH value did not adjusted. The light source was provided by a 300W Xe lamp with a 400nm cutoff filter. 20mg as-prepared photocatalysts were transferred into a Pyrex photocatalytic reactor contained with 100mL, 10mgL−1 organic pollutant with continuous stirring. An air pump was employed to enable a constant supplying of oxygen. Experiments were carried out at 30°C by using a circulating water system to prevent thermal catalytic effects. The mixed solution was stirred for 30min in the darkness to achieve the adsorption-desorption equilibrium, and then 3mL suspension was sampled and centrifuged to obtain clear liquid at certain time intervals under visible light irradiation. A UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2450, Shimadzu) was used to analyzed the concentration of RhB with the characteristic band at 553nm.

Bi4O5I2/ITO and 1wt% CQDs/Bi4O5I2/ITO were served as working electrode, respectively, with a platinum wire as counter electrode and a saturated Ag/AgCl electrode as reference electrode to investigate the separation and transfer of photo-generated carriers of prepared samples. Photocurrent tests were performed on an electrochemical working station (CHI660B, Chen Hua Instruments, Shanghai, China) equipped with a 500W xenon lamp. The photocurrent and EIS were performed in a 0.1M phosphate buffered saline aqueous solution (pH=7) and a 0.1M KCl solution containing 5mM Fe(CN)6 3−/Fe(CN)6 4−, respectively.

The phase structures and crystallinity of the pure Bi4O5I2 and CQDs/Bi4O5I2 materials were analyzed by XRD technique. As shown in Fig. 1 , the distinctive peaks at 2θ=28.8°, 31.5°, 36.9°, 42.1°, 45.1°, 49.3° and 54.4° could be indexed to diffraction peaks of the obtained samples, corresponding to the (−4−11), (402), ( −404), (−323), (422), (006) and (811) crystal faces. The results were consistent with the previous literature [31,36]. No characteristic peaks for CQDs power located around 26° [37] could be detected in the CQDs/Bi4O5I2 materials, which may be attributed to the low content of CQDs in the CQDs/Bi4O5I2 materials.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) analysis was measured to confirm the existence of CQDs in as-prepared CQDs/Bi4O5I2 samples, the results were presented in Fig. 2 . The peak at 1534cm−1 was attributed to the bending vibrations of NH, 1562cm−1 belonged to stretching vibrating of CC, meanwhile peaks at 1651 and 1700cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of CO in ketone and amido, respectively [35,38]. No obvious characteristic peaks assigned to imidazolium could be found in the FT-IR spectrogram, which strongly suggested that ionic liquids can be easily removed from as-obtained samples. These results primely indicated that CQDs have been successfully introduced into the Bi4O5I2 nanomaterials.

Fig. 3 were the XPS survey spectra and high-resolution XPS spectra of the Bi 4f, I 3d and O 1s for Bi4O5I2 as well as CQDs/Bi4O5I2 samples and C 1s for CQDs/Bi4O5I2 sample, which were used to investigate the chemical composition and states of materials. In the full survey spectrum (Fig. 3a), it can be seen that the main component elements for Bi4O5I2 and CQDs/Bi4O5I2 are Bi, I, O and C. In the high-resolution Bi 4f spectra (Fig. 3b), the peaks at 159.1 and 164.3eV were belong to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2, respectively [22]. Two typical peaks located at 619.1 and 630.6eV could be assigned to I 3d3/2 and I 3d5/2, respectively, in Fig. 3c I 3d spectra of the samples [39]. As shown in Fig. 3d, the peak binding energy of 529.9eV was attributed to O 1s [36]. The C 1s peaks located at 284.8, 286.5 and 288.7eV in Fig. 3e were attributed to the CC bond with sp2 orbital, the oxygenated carbon and nitrous carbon, respectively [8,40]. The XPS results were in accordance with above-mentioned FT-IR analysis results.

The morphology of the CQDs/Bi4O5I2 materials was observed under a transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As can be seen in Fig. 4 , the prepared Bi4O5I2 nanomaterials were sheet-like structure and the thickness of Bi4O5I2 nanomaterials was about 6nm. From Fig. 4b and c, it showed some dark dots with average size about 5nm were distributed on the slightly larger nanosheets, suggesting the CQDs were deposited on the surface of Bi4O5I2 nanosheets.

The elemental mapping measure was taken to further determine the distribution of CQDs on the Bi4O5I2 nanosheets, and the results were shown in Fig. 5 . The analysis demonstrated that the CQDs/Bi4O5I2 material was composed of the elements Bi, C, I and O, while these four elements were evenly distributed in the material. These results strongly proved that CQDs have been loaded on the surface of Bi4O5I2 nanosheets, and that was coincident with the TEM analysis.

The nitrogen absorption-desorption isotherms were carried out to determine the BET surface areas of the obtained samples. Fig. 8 exhibited the specific surface areas of Bi4O5I2, 0.5wt%, 1wt% and 3wt% CQDs/Bi4O5I2, which were calculated to be 38.12, 44.12, 52.60, 74.03m2 g−1, respectively. It is the universal recognition that more exposed specific surface area is conducive to provide a good contact between catalysts and pollutants and absorb more active species on the surface for the photocatalytic degradation process [5]. In this system, noticeable higher specific surface area of CQDs/Bi4O5I2 materials revealing the CQDs would work as the absorption sites. As a result, the superior photocatalytic performance can be expected.

To evaluate the photocatalytic performance of CQDs/Bi4O5I2 samples, rhodamine B (RhB) was selected as the target pollutant for the photocatalytic degradation experiment under visible light irradiation. Prior to the photocatalytic reaction, adsorption-desorption equilibrium was achieved between the pollutant and photocatalyst with continuous stirring for 30min in the darkness. As shown in Fig. 9 , the photocatalytic activities of the CQDs/Bi4O5I2 materials improved significantly by compared with pure Bi4O5I2 sample. After visible light irradiation for 30min, the degrees of RhB degradation for Bi4O5I2 sample was about 18%, in the meantime, 1wt% CQDs/Bi4O5I2 material displayed the highest photocatalytic activity among the materials and more than 60% of RhB has been removed. The removal rate of RhB reached about 94% for 1wt% CQDs/Bi4O5I2 material while only 76% for the pure Bi4O5I2 sample after 120min visible light irradiation. Apparently, the degradation rate of RhB was obviously decreased when the amount of CQDs was over 1wt%, which could be ascribed to that too many CQDs loaded on the surface of Bi4O5I2 limited the Bi4O5I2 to absorb more visible light.

Fig. 10 was the photocatalytic degradation kinetics of RhB among as-obtained samples under visible light. The reaction kinetics of the RhB degradation over Bi4O5I2 and CQDs/Bi4O5I2 materials could be expressed exactly by a pseudo-first-order kinetic model through the equation of –ln (C/C 0)= kt. It could be observed distinctly that 1wt% CQDs/Bi4O5I2 sample possessed the maximum reaction rate constant of 0.0335min−1, which was 3.2 times of the pure Bi4O5I2 (0.0104min−1).

The yield and availability of the photogenerated electrons and holes play crucial roles in the generation of free radical species and degradation of pollutants throughout the photocatalytic reaction. Herein, photoluminescence (PL) technique was applied to investigated the transportation and recombination process of photo-introduced electron-hole pairs in the pure Bi4O5I2 and 1wt% CQDs/Bi4O5I2 materials during the process of photocatalysis under visible light. As can be seen in Fig. 11 , the 1wt% CQDs/Bi4O5I2 material exhibited much lower intensity than the pure Bi4O5I2 sample, which implied that the introduction of CQDs could significantly suppress the recombination rate of the photogenerated charge carriers [41]. The results could be attributed to the high-efficiency surface electron transfer from Bi4O5I2 to CQDs, and the highly efficient electron transfer could be one of the major factors that induced the enhanced photocatalytic performance.

The electrochemistry analysis method was employed to further explore the electron separation rate in the Bi4O5I2 sample and transfer process on the surface from Bi4O5I2 to CQDs. The transient photocurrent responses of the pure Bi4O5I2 and 1wt% CQDs/Bi4O5I2 samples were measured under visible light with several on-off cycle events. It can be observed distinctly in Fig. 12 a, the CQDs/Bi4O5I2 material exhibited the strongest photocurrent intensity, which was roughly quadruple than the Bi4O5I2 sample. The much stronger photocurrent intensity could be assigned to the stronger ability in the production of charge carriers and effective transfer of electrons on the interfaces of Bi4O5I2 and CQDs for CQDs/Bi4O5I2 material [42]. Fig. 12b was the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) for Bi4O5I2 and CQDs/Bi4O5I2 samples. The diameter of the Nyquist circle for the 1wt% CQDs/Bi4O5I2 was smaller as compared with Bi4O5I2, that implies 1wt% CQDs/Bi4O5I2 possessed a low resistance value and the introduction of CQDs to Bi4O5I2 could induce a faster transfer for interfacial charges so as to enhance the photocatalytic performance [43].

The active species produced during the photocatalytic reaction process over CQDs/Bi4O5I2 sample under visible light were detected using a electron spin resonance (ESR) spin-trap technique without or with irradiation, the results were exhibited in Fig. 13 . The characteristic peaks of superoxide free radical (O2
−) and hydroxyl free radical (OH
) could be observed by adding 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as scavenger with methanol dispersion and aqueous dispersion, respectively. No signals in both ESR spectra could be detected in dark environments. Interestingly, four characteristic peaks of DMPO-O2
− that could be seen apparently with light on shown in Fig. 13a, that has not appeared for pure Bi4O5I2 materials. It can be reasonably surmised that the CQDs modification caused the effective utilization of high-energy electrons to take part in the reduction of O2. At the same time, it also can be observed that four strong characteristic peaks of DMPO-OH appeared in Fig. 13b under visible light irradiation. Based on the previous researches that have been reported, no characteristic peaks of OH
could be detected in BiOI system [8]. The OH
generated over Bi4O5I2 material could be attributed to the changed band positions regulated by the appropriate dehalogenation. Thus, the results displayed that both O2
− and OH
radical species could be generated concurrently over CQDs/Bi4O5I2 system and also acted as the main active species during the photocatalytic procedure. However, it can be observed that the intensity of characteristic peaks for OH
radicals was higher than that of O2
− radicals. Hence, it implied that OH
radicals may play a more significant role than O2
− radicals in the photocatalytic reaction.

In order to further determine the roles that the active species acted in the photocatalytic degradation reaction, different free radicals trapping experiments of 3wt% CQDs/Bi4O5I2 material with visible light irradiation have been carried out. As shown in Fig. 14 , the triethanolamine and benzoquinone were chosen as a trapping agent for trapping holes (h+) and O2
−, respectively. As expected, when the triethanolamine was added into the suspension, there was nearly no photocatalytic degradation efficiency of pollutant that could be observed, which indicated the hole was the main active specie. At the same time the photocatalytic degradation efficiency declined 60%, it demonstrated that superoxide radical played a crucial role in the degradation process as well, and the result was in accordance with the ESR analysis. Based on the ESR analysis and free radicals trapping experiment results, it can be confirmed that O2
−, OH
and h+ radical species played coordinating roles during the procedure of degrading pollutants under visible light irradiation, and the excellent photocatalytic ability could be achieved.

On the strength of the formulas E VB = X − E e +0.5E g and E CB = E VB − E g, where X is the electronegativity of the corresponding semiconductor, E e is the energy of free electrons on the hydrogen scale, the valence band potentials (E VB) and the conduction band potential (E CB) of Bi4O5I2 sample can be estimated. The X value of Bi4O5I2 was calculated to be 5.93eV and E e was 4.5eV. In the meantime, the E g of the Bi4O5I2 sample was determined to be approximately 2.21eV as above mentioned [31]. As a consequence, the E VB and the E CB of Bi4O5I2 catalyst were estimated to be 2.54 and 0.33eV, respectively. We know that, the reduction potentials of
OH/OH− and O2/O2
− were 2.38eV vs NHE and −0.046eV vs NHE, respectively [36]. Based on the above calculated results, it could be assumed that the Bi4O5I2 oxidizing the surface OH− group to generate
OH could be achievable, while trapping molecular oxygen to generate O2
− was impracticable. This obtained conclusions were discrepant with the above ESR analysis and free radicals trapping experiments results. The cause of this result could be attributed to the CQDs modified on the surface of Bi4O5I2 possessed remarkable ability of interfacial electron transfer so that electrons occupied in high-energy potential on the CB of Bi4O5I2 transferred to CQDs to inhibit the recombination of photo-induced electron-hole pairs. Thus, the higher concentration of hydroxyl radicals could be obtained in the CQDs/Bi4O5I2 system than pure Bi4O5I2 material while superoxide radicals also be produced in the CQDs/Bi4O5I2 system which did not find in the Bi4O5I2 material to participate in the photodegradation reaction under visible light irradiation, which confirmed the above conjectures. The proposed mechanism was exhibited in Fig. 15 .

As a whole, novel CQDs/Bi4O5I2 materials have been synthesized via a solvothermal method. CQDs modified on the surface of Bi4O5I2 nanomaterials, with average size about 5nm, acted as the transfer sites for photogenerated high-energy electrons, absorption sites for pollutant and reaction sites for photocatalytic degradation simultaneously. A halogen and CQDs bidirectional regulation strategy was first proposed in this study. The proper changed band structure of BiOI material and the high-efficient transfer of high-energy electrons on the CB of Bi4O5I2 material result in the main active species were h+, OH
and O2
− during the photocatalytic reaction under visible irradiation. And the 1wt% CQDs/Bi4O5I2 material exhibited the highest photocatalytic activity.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21476098, 21471069 and 21576123), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2013M541619), Science and Technology support program of Zhenjiang (SH2014018, NY2014021), and the Science and technology support plan of Dantu (SH2015002).
